tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Create an ECR repository in your AWS account via the AWS console or AWS CLI. Retrieve the repository URI and verify the format matches '<account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>'.
tasks.starter:
# Option A — AWS Console:
# 1. Open the ECR service in the AWS console
# 2. Create repository → name it 'myapp' → Create
# 3. Copy the repository URI shown

# Option B — AWS CLI:
aws ecr create-repository \
  --repository-name myapp \
  --region <your-region>
# The output includes 'repositoryUri' — copy it.
tasks.solution:
# AWS CLI example (region: us-east-1, account: 123456789012):
aws ecr create-repository \
  --repository-name myapp \
  --region us-east-1

# Expected JSON output (relevant excerpt):
# {
#   "repository": {
#     "repositoryName": "myapp",
#     "repositoryUri": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp",
#     ...
#   }
# }

# Verify format:
# <account-id>   : 12-digit AWS account number
# <region>       : e.g. us-east-1, ap-northeast-2
# <repo-name>    : myapp

# List repositories to confirm creation:
aws ecr describe-repositories --region us-east-1
tasks.explanation: ECR repository URIs carry the full routing information for Docker: when you 'docker push' to a URI that begins with an AWS account ID and region, the Docker client knows to authenticate to that specific ECR endpoint. This is why the tag on your image must include the full URI, not just the image name — Docker uses the tag as the address.
hints: If 'aws ecr create-repository' returns 'Unable to locate credentials', your AWS CLI is not configured. Run 'aws configure' and provide your access key ID, secret access key, and default region.
If you see 'RepositoryAlreadyExistsException', the repository was already created — run 'aws ecr describe-repositories' to find and copy the existing URI.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Authenticate the Docker client on the Jenkins agent to ECR, then manually push a locally built image to the ECR repository. Verify the image appears in the ECR console with the correct tag.
tasks.starter:
# Replace placeholders with your values:
REGION=<your-region>
ACCOUNT=<your-account-id>
REGISTRY=${ACCOUNT}.dkr.ecr.${REGION}.amazonaws.com

# Step 1: Authenticate Docker to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region ${REGION} \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${REGISTRY}

# Step 2: Tag the image already built in Lecture 5
docker tag myapp:1 ${REGISTRY}/myapp:<tag>

# Step 3: Push
docker push ${REGISTRY}/myapp:<tag>
tasks.solution:
REGION=us-east-1
ACCOUNT=123456789012
REGISTRY=${ACCOUNT}.dkr.ecr.${REGION}.amazonaws.com

# Step 1: Authenticate
aws ecr get-login-password --region ${REGION} \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${REGISTRY}
# Expected output: 'Login Succeeded'

# Step 2: Get the commit SHA to use as tag
COMMIT=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
# e.g. COMMIT=abc1234

# Step 3: Tag the local image
docker tag myapp:1 ${REGISTRY}/myapp:${COMMIT}

# Step 4: Push
docker push ${REGISTRY}/myapp:${COMMIT}
# Output: lists digest and shows pushed layers

# Verification:
# Open AWS console → ECR → myapp repository
# You see the image listed with tag 'abc1234' and a pushed timestamp.
# OR via CLI:
aws ecr describe-images \
  --repository-name myapp \
  --region ${REGION}
tasks.explanation: ECR authentication is a temporary token flow: 'aws ecr get-login-password' contacts the AWS API using your IAM credentials and returns a short-lived (12-hour) password. Piping it into 'docker login --password-stdin' authenticates Docker without ever printing the password in the terminal or build log. The username is always the literal string 'AWS' for any ECR registry. Using the commit SHA as the tag creates a permanent, bidirectional link: given the running image tag, you can find the exact commit; given the commit, you know which image it produced.
hints: If 'docker login' fails with 'no basic auth credentials', the 'aws ecr get-login-password' step may have failed silently (e.g. wrong region or missing IAM permissions). Run 'aws ecr get-login-password --region <region>' alone first and confirm it outputs a token string.

If the push fails with 'denied: User ... is not authorized to perform: ecr:InitiateLayerUpload', your IAM user/role needs the 'AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPowerUser' policy (or at minimum ecr:GetAuthorizationToken, ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability, ecr:PutImage, ecr:InitiateLayerUpload, ecr:UploadLayerPart, ecr:CompleteLayerUpload).
If you get 'no space left on device' during the push, the Jenkins agent's disk is full — run 'docker system prune' to remove unused images and layers.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Add a 'Push to ECR' stage to the Jenkinsfile. The stage should: retrieve the Git commit SHA, authenticate to ECR, retag the image built in the previous 'Build Image' stage with the commit SHA, push it to ECR, and optionally push a second tag matching the branch name as a convenience pointer.
tasks.explanation: The 'script' block inside a declarative Jenkinsfile allows Groovy scripting. 'sh(script: ..., returnStdout: true).trim()' captures the standard output of a shell command and removes trailing whitespace, giving you the commit SHA as a Groovy string variable. The dual-tag strategy (commit SHA + branch name) satisfies two needs: the commit SHA tag is immutable and gives you a precise rollback target; the branch tag is a mutable pointer to the latest image on that branch, useful for deployment scripts that always want 'the newest on main'.
hints: If 'git rev-parse --short HEAD' returns 'HEAD' instead of a SHA inside the pipeline, the checkout step may have used a detached HEAD. Replace with 'env.GIT_COMMIT?.take(7)' which Jenkins populates from the triggering commit.

If the push succeeds for the commit tag but fails for the branch tag with 'image already exists', that is fine — the branch tag may already point to the same image layers; Docker will update the tag without re-uploading layers.
The 'withCredentials' wrapper masks secrets in the build log. Never pass AWS keys via environment variables set directly in the Jenkinsfile — they would appear in the pipeline configuration UI.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge: You receive a bug report: 'A container running in production with tag latest crashed, and we cannot identify which code version it came from.' Explain in writing (in your exercise notes or a comment block in the Jenkinsfile) why the 'latest' tag caused this situation, and demonstrate the correct approach by showing what the 'docker images' and ECR repository would look like after three builds using the commit-SHA tagging strategy instead.
tasks.explanation: This challenge enforces the tagging discipline introduced in the lecture. The inability to identify a running image's source code is a real, recurring production problem when 'latest' is used in pipelines. Commit-SHA tags transform the image registry into an audit trail: every deployment is traceable, every rollback is immediate (no rebuild needed), and no version is ever silently overwritten. The immutability guarantee also means a security scan of 'myapp:a1b2c3d' applies to that exact image forever — 'latest' can be rescanned against a different image each time.
hints: To check what code a running container was built from (with SHA tags): run 'docker inspect <container-name>' and look at the 'Image' field. The SHA in the tag maps directly to a git commit you can inspect with 'git log <sha>'.
ECR allows you to enable 'image tag immutability' on a repository, which prevents overwriting an existing tag. Enable this in production to enforce the tagging discipline at the registry level.
